headline: Jean-Michel Basquiat: The Market That Refuses to Cool
keyStats: Versus Medici (1982): $50.4M at Sotheby's, 2021 — all-time record
instagram:
Jean-Michel Basquiat: The Market That Refuses to Cool

Few artists command the auction room the way Basquiat does. A look at the data tells the story.

In 2010, his works averaged $2.5M at auction. By 2013 — the year Dustheads (1982) shattered expectations at $48.8M at Christie's — the market had tripled to a $7M average. That sale alone announced Basquiat as a blue-chip cornerstone of the contemporary art market.

Then came 2021. With «Versus Medici» (1982) fetching $50.4M at Sotheby's, annual sales volume hit a staggering $326M — the highest single year on record. The average sale that year? $16.3M. That's a 550% rise in average sale price from 2010 to 2021.

Yes, 2022 saw a pullback — the broader market cooling post-pandemic — but the fundamentals held. By 2023, Self-Portrait As A Heel (Part Two) (1982) sold for $42M, and 2024 brought Untitled (Elmar) (1982) in at $46.5M at Phillips. Averages stabilized around the $10–11M range.

And 2025? Already $164M in total volume with Crowns (Peso Neto) (1981) leading at $48.3M — signaling the top of the market is very much intact.

The thesis is simple: Basquiat's 1981–1982 paintings are generational assets. His work sits at the intersection of art history, cultural identity, and raw collector demand. Every dip has been a buying opportunity. Every peak has set a new floor.

The market isn't speculating on Basquiat anymore. It's pricing in permanence.

#Basquiat #ArtMarket #BlueChipArt #Masterworks #ContemporaryArt #AlternativeInvestments
linkedin:
A decade of auction data on Jean-Michel Basquiat surfaces a market thesis that's hard to ignore.

**The Basquiat market has delivered +550% growth in average sale price since 2010 — and the 2022 correction may already be behind us.**

Key data points from the auction record:

• 2010: avg sale $2.5M | total volume $68.6M
• 2013: *Dustheads* (1982) sells for $48.8M at Christie's — avg jumps to $7M
• 2021: *«Versus Medici»* (1982) reaches $50.4M — annual volume peaks at $326M, avg $16.3M
• 2022: volume contracts to $66.6M as broader market corrects
• 2023–2024: recovery — *Self-Portrait As A Heel (Part Two)* at $42M, *Untitled (Elmar)* at $46.5M — avg stabilizes at $10–11M
• 2025 YTD: $164M total volume, *Crowns (Peso Neto)* (1981) at $48.3M

**What the data suggests about the road ahead:**

Basquiat's 1981–1982 works consistently represent the top of his market — a narrow, highly liquid segment where global collector demand remains deep. The post-2021 normalization looks increasingly like a consolidation rather than a structural break: top-lot prices have held above $40M, and 2025 volume is tracking toward one of his strongest years on record.

The correction absorbed macro headwinds — rising rates, a risk-off shift in alternative assets — without meaningfully eroding the price floor for museum-quality works. That resilience is the signal.

For collectors and allocators watching this market: the data doesn't point to a bubble deflating. It points to a market finding its new equilibrium at a significantly higher base than a decade ago.
